name: M.S. in Chemical Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The four lecture courses shown are exactly the canonical graduate chemical-engineering core: Thermodynamics (CHE 611), Kinetics and Reactor Design (CHE 612), Transport Phenomena I (CHE 624), and Mathematical Methods (CHE 626), complemented by research vehicles (Master's Project CHE 700B, Master's Thesis CHE 701B/C) and the seminar (CHE 791). This is a coherent, standard core; the limitation is that no electives or specialization tracks appear among the 8 referenced courses and requirementGroups is 0, so the elective/depth structure of the 30-credit degree is invisible.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the elective menu and any concentration tracks (e.g., pharmaceutical, materials, energy, catalysis) alongside the core so the full 30-credit path is legible.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is clean: missingCourseCount 0 (no dangling references) and prerequisites are sensibly stated as the required undergraduate background ('undergraduate courses in fluid mechanics, heat transfer, and mass transfer' for CHE 624), appropriate for an M.S. admitting B.S. ChemE graduates. deepestPrereqChain is 0 only because the sampled set is the core; advanced electives (which exist in the department, e.g., CHE 709/710) would add depth.
dimensions.suggestion: If advanced electives such as Advanced Separations or a Transport II exist, list them with their coded prerequisites so the progression depth beyond the core is visible.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are published, a real gap for an M.S. catalog page where prospective students and assessment reviewers expect stated graduate competencies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish program learning outcomes (e.g., formulate and solve transport/reaction models, design experiments, communicate research) mapped to the core courses.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is 30, which is the standard M.S. ChemE load and already more transparent than most sibling programs, and the visible thesis (CHE 701B 3 cr, 701C 6 cr) and project (CHE 700B 3 cr) options signal thesis and non-thesis paths. creditReconcileDelta is null because no plan groups were parsed, so the internal core-versus-elective distribution is not shown.
dimensions.suggestion: Show the credit split (e.g., about 12 credits of core, elective credits, and a 3- or 6-credit thesis or project) and clarify the thesis (CHE 701B/C) versus project (CHE 700B) versus coursework-only tracks.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The four core courses have concise, specific descriptions, and the total credit count (30) is stated. However the research and seminar entries are administrative, CHE 791's description begins with a stray ', 1 contact hour' parsing artifact, and there is no plan-of-study table or labeled terms.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean the CHE 791 description (leading-comma artifact) and add a plan-of-study table showing core, electives, and the research options against the 30 credits.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The core is durable and relevant, and CHE 612 explicitly emphasizes 'small-scale pharmaceutical production,' aligning with New Jersey's pharma sector. But the sampled set exposes no modern electives (process data science/ML, sustainability/decarbonization, process safety, electrochemical/energy), so demonstrated currency depends on unlisted courses.
dimensions.suggestion: Surface modern electives such as process data analytics/machine learning, decarbonization/electrification, and process safety to make the program's currency visible on the page.

descriptionQuality.justification: The core course descriptions (CHE 611, 612, 624, 626) are specific and informative, but the research/seminar entries are administrative and CHE 791 carries a leading-comma parsing artifact, so aggregate quality is good but uneven.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are consistently stated as the required undergraduate background with no dangling references (missingCourseCount 0), which is sensible; clarity is limited by their prose form and by there being no coded graduate prerequisites among the shown core.

missingTopics.topic: Process systems engineering: optimization, control, and data-driven modeling
missingTopics.rationale: Beyond Mathematical Methods (CHE 626), no advanced optimization, process control, or systems course is visible, a central competency of modern ChemE.

missingTopics.topic: Data science and machine learning for chemical engineering
missingTopics.rationale: The sibling materials program lists MTEN 631/633, but the ChemE core exposes no data-science/ML course despite its growing role in process development.
No process-safety or sustainability course appears, though both are now core to chemical-process practice and accreditation.
Department courses like CHE 709/710 (separations) and CHE 750/756 (catalysis) are not surfaced in this program's course set, leaving specialization depth hidden.
Energy storage/conversion and electrochemical engineering, central to the energy transition, are absent from the visible curriculum.

emergingTrends.trend: AI/ML and hybrid data-driven process modeling
emergingTrends.rationale: Machine learning and hybrid first-principles/data models are transforming process design and operations and should appear as an elective.
The energy transition is reshaping chemical processing; a decarbonization/carbon-capture elective would modernize the program.
Building on CHE 612's pharmaceutical emphasis, continuous manufacturing and bioprocessing are high-growth directions for NJ industry.
Intensified, modular process design is a current practice trend that transport/reactor courses could incorporate.

emergingTrends.trend: Digital twins and Industry 4.0 for process plants
emergingTrends.rationale: Real-time process digital twins and sensor-driven operations are emerging skills relevant to the transport and control curriculum.

topSuggestions: Publish the full plan of study: core plus the elective menu, concentration tracks, and thesis/project options mapped to the 30 credits.
State program learning outcomes and map them to the core courses.
Add or surface a data-science/machine-learning-for-ChemE elective and a process-safety/sustainability course.
Expose advanced elective sequences (separations, catalysis, molecular simulation) so progression depth beyond the core is visible.
Clean presentation artifacts (CHE 791) and clarify the thesis versus project versus coursework-only tracks.
